title: The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Findings from consultation 15 July to 9 August 2024 | Stats NZ
content:
## The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Findings from consultation 15 July to 9 August 2024
08 October 2024, 1:30pm
The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Findings from consultation 15 July to 9 August 2024 summarises the findings from submissions, and explains the processes used to collect and analyse feedback on The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Consultation .
It also provides information on the next steps we will take in updating the framework for occupation.
Background on the consultation Summary Methodology Findings Next steps Glossary Appendixes

## Background on the consultation
The Australian and New Zealand Standard Classification of Occupations (ANZSCO) is used to classify all occupations and jobs. This standard is jointly managed by Stats NZ and the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS).
ANZSCO is significantly outdated and no longer fit for purpose for New Zealand or Australia. Since it was released in 2006, the labour market and economy have changed drastically, and the classification does not accurately reflect the contemporary reality in either country.
The Government Statistician and the Australian Statistician have discussed the proposed changes to the current ownership model of this classification and have agreed to support the development of classifications that address the reality in each country.
Stats NZ has consulted to understand stakeholder needs and the implications of different options. The ABS has also been reviewing ANZSCO with the intention to better meet the needs of the Australian labour market. Our timing and approach to consultation in New Zealand has been informed by Australia’s review.
We sought feedback on:
- Part A: Options for an updated occupation classification and our intention to introduce independent but aligned occupation classifications for Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia
- Part B: Implementation considerations, concerns, and mitigations.
Appendix 1 outlines the options in Part A.
We received a total of 91 completed submissions during the four-week consultation period, including:
- 37 from industry representatives and Workforce Development Councils
- 24 from government agencies
Of the feedback received:
- 80 submissions supported option 1 (88 percent)
- 7 submissions did not express support for any option (8 percent)
- 3 submissions did not support option 1 (3 percent).
The three submitters who did not support option 1 wanted to retain ANZSCO (or a version of ANZSCO) to ensure comparability across New Zealand and Australia.
We had overwhelming support from organisations, government departments, and businesses who agreed with introducing a New Zealand-focused classification, as set out in the consultation paper. We received many suggestions for where improvements could be made, as well as some criticism of the approach.
Next steps summarises how we will address feedback.
The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Consultation has the public consultation documentation.
Stats NZ Tatauranga Aotearoa consulted on the future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand.
The public consultation process has provided a wealth of information to inform the direction for the future occupation classification. Overall, there was strong support for the proposal to have a tailored occupation classification for New Zealand whilst maintaining comparability with Australia from a range of stakeholder groups. While views differed in some areas, submitters tended to view the proposal as a positive step forward from the current joint occupation classification (ANZSCO).
Other feedback received through submissions related to the dated nature of ANZSCO and a desire for a change to a new approach. Other high-level themes were:
Continued alignment with Australia
Ensuring the continuity and comparability of occupation classification data was seen as essential, particularly for maintaining alignment with Australia and facilitating cross-border relations between New Zealand and Australia.
More visibility of relevant occupations in the data
Submissions highlighted the need for more visibility of occupations in the classification. Some occupations have alternative titles or specialisations in the current ANZSCO, which leads to misunderstandings on how to code an occupation with a different title. The gap in Māori occupations was also highlighted.
Submitters identified factors relevant to data quality. The proposed classification offers more occupation titles that match the New Zealand job market, including kaupapa Māori occupations, which will in turn lead to better data capture and higher-quality outputs.
Why occupation data is important
Many submitters talked about the importance of accurate occupation data, particularly with knowing where the gaps are in the job market so industries can look at building their work force to address these shortages.
This section outlines the process used to collect public submissions, and the methodology to analyse them.

### Consultation process
The future of occupation classifications in Aotearoa New Zealand: Consultation outlined proposed changes to classifying occupation data.
We asked for feedback on proposed changes and invited submissions by email or by meeting with the Stats NZ team. Consultation began on 15 July 2024 and was open for four weeks. Invitations to the consultation were emailed to government agencies and industry representatives, as well as groups and individuals who had previously engaged with us. The consultation was also available to the public via the Stats NZ website and social media channels. Late submissions were accepted on request for one week following the close of the online form on 9 August 2024.
Stats NZ held two information sessions, online via Microsoft Teams, to give a more in-depth overview of the consultation document and to answer any questions. Twenty-three people attended, representing 16 businesses, organisations, and government agencies. We also invited any stakeholders to meet with us individually if they wanted to discuss how the proposal would directly affect their organisation. We met with 15 organisations and government agencies.
The following section outlines the analyses of the feedback and summarises the themes and issues identified in the submissions we received during the four-week consultation period. Submissions received during the consultation process were qualitatively analysed and categorised into themes and sub-themes.

### How we analysed submissions
The analysis used qualitative manual coding, where two analysts coded all submission responses and identified common themes. Each analyst worked independently, and the outcomes were reanalysed to identify the final list of major themes and corresponding sub-themes. This thematic analysis allowed us to categorise the main ideas in submissions.
Appendix 2 lists the themes and sub-themes we identified in our analysis.
The majority (88 percent) of submissions agreed the current joint Australia and New Zealand occupation classification was not fully meeting the needs of those who use it. They agreed that moving to an independent but aligned (with Australia) occupation classification would better fit the needs of business, industry, and government in New Zealand.
The findings are presented as themes and sub-themes, with quotes from submissions to give context on issues that have been identified. We’ve removed any part of a quote that could identify an individual but have included any references to organisations or agencies. The use of a quote does not imply or represent the expression of any opinion by Stats NZ.
Some submissions were on a topic related to, but not directly about, the occupation classification. These were considered out of scope of the current work but were noted for future analysis.

### Part A: Options for an updated occupation classification and our intention to introduce independent but aligned occupation classifications for Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia
We asked submitters if they supported or did not support the proposal to introduce an independent but aligned occupation classification for New Zealand and Australia, and for their preferred option for an updated occupation classification.
The following four options were provided with the consultation document:
- Option 1: Create an independent New Zealand occupation classification that leverages existing content
- Option 2: Adopt the Australian revision to ANZSCO v1.3 (currently under development)
- Option 3: Adapt the Australian revision to ANZSCO v1.3 to reflect New Zealand content in a joint classification
- Option 4: Update the current version of ANZSCO v1.3 for New Zealand-only classification.
Appendix 1 has more detail on each of the options.
Themes and sub-themes from submissions are outlined below.

#### Agreement with the proposal and adoption of option 1
The majority of submitters (88 percent) agreed with the proposal, and their feedback had the following themes:
Submitters strongly supported a move away from maintaining a joint classification because the Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia have different needs, economic drivers in the labour markets, job titles, and skills.
Submitters recognised that maintaining joint classifications was a “complex, challenging, and expensive current reality”.
While there was strong support for an independent classification, submitters made it clear that maintaining alignment with Australia was highly important.
Here is an example from feedback:
We underscore the need for any separate New Zealand classification to remain meaningfully aligned to the Australian classification via concordances that can permit users to accurately compare occupations or groups of occupations between the two countries.
Submitters recognised the need for the new classification to be inclusive of all New Zealand occupations. Industry and business representatives were particularly supportive of a New Zealand-focused classification that accurately reflected the job roles and titles that are important to them. This would improve the data by making occupations not currently in the classification visible. The ability for organisations and people to find an occupation that reflects what they do is important for representation.
We believe that developing more suitable classifications for the New Zealand labour market will allow for more representative insights to inform New Zealanders for the best possible decision-making.
We recognise the challenges of joint classifications, particularly in updating lists or adding new occupations. To address these challenges, we see a significant benefit in developing a separate but aligned occupational classification system tailored to New Zealand’s labour market.
Some submitters highlighted creating an independent New Zealand occupation classification (option 1) as an opportunity to improve data quality.
If the existing occupation codes were improved there is the potential for more effective analytical interrogations of the data that would provide a more insightful breakdown of workforce characteristics such as occupations by: income levels/ranges, demographic compositions, those [that] are impacted by adverse events and career progressions/pathways being more trackable etc.
From an operational perspective, having a relatively more New Zealand-centric set of occupation descriptors should improve the quality of coded job vacancies and analysis of trends in the domestic labour market over time.
Several submitters noted that a key feature of option 1 was the ability to include Māori-specific occupations in the new classification. Submitters identified this as a gap in the current proposed classification and noted that a significant amount of time would be needed to engage appropriately to understand this kaupapa.
The classification needs to bring kaupapa Māori jobs to the forefront of insights, giving further value to a range of Māori job pathways and the work that Māori do, some of which are not necessarily on an employment contract but fulfil their cultural duties for their hapori, whānau, hapū, and iwi, to live as Māori and as citizens of Aotearoa.
A te ao Māori lens is important to our future system. An example would [be] the role of Kaitiaki, which is utilised in a nursery environment using [a] Mat[a]uranga Māori approach. More visibility of kaupapa Māori occupations would help us.
One such gap within the current classifications is [the] inclusion of the unique offering of te ao Māori and kaupapa Māori occupations, particularly for toi Māori.
Aligned with the inclusion of more Māori occupations, translating the classification to te reo Māori was also a common request.
We would also like Stats NZ to develop a comprehensive set of job titles in te reo Māori, as well as the synonyms. This will help all of the government data systems to have a consistency in te reo Māori, rather than each agency to have a bespoke process. This will be the corpus of job titles that anyone in Aotearoa can use for a wide range of products.
Alongside support for creating an independent New Zealand occupation classification some submitters made specific comments on how immigration processes could be affected or improved.
[There will be] greater visibility of occupations that are on Immigration NZ’s Green List – for example, endodontists.
[We will be] better able to find the occupation code and skill level that match actual jobs.
If the new classification creates more specific roles (with their own unique ANZSCO codes) without ensuring that these roles are also reflected in the Green List, it could inadvertently exclude professionals who were previously eligible for skilled migration. This would have a direct impact on our ability to advise clients and on the eligibility of skilled migrants.
The Green List is a list of roles (produced by Immigration NZ) that are currently in demand in New Zealand.
It was noted that the proposed changes to an independent New Zealand occupation classification would not impact Immigration NZ’s needs. It was noted that it could simplify their processes by removing the need for exemptions.
The proposed new approach will continue to meet Immigration New Zealand needs – it will provide clear classification of occupations, along with role descriptions and skill levels. Being more responsive to changes in the labour market and feedback from stakeholders is a good thing. We hear a lot of noise from stakeholders that they don’t think the classification of their occupation is right and we sometimes have to make exemptions in immigration instructions as a result (eg, for certain roles to be recognised as higher skill level so that they can access better immigration settings).
Submitters responded positively to linking to Tahatū.
- utilising connecting National Certificate of Educational Achievement (NCEA) school subjects, qualifications, and careers
- showing the most common pathways others have taken to get to a career or job.
We see a great merit and opportunity for the future occupation classifications linking with Tahatū, as long as Stats NZ meaningfully engages with Māori communities for the classification, rather than just importing Tahatū data.
Tahatū, developed by the Tertiary Education Commission, is currently in pilot at a select number of secondary schools in Aotearoa, linking NCEA school subjects with qualifications and careers. Linking a new occupation classification with this resource will help broaden the career pathways where we can connect to TEC’s application where there is a good potential of finding career paths and tertiary education details in a single site.
[We] agree with [the] proposal to use digital technology to link classifications to the content and information on Tahatū and avoid duplication.

#### Continuity and comparability
The importance of concordances was highlighted in many submissions.
Concordances provide the mapping of one classification (or version) to another. Concordances for data continuity and time-series analysis will be maintained by Stats NZ. These will exist from the current ANZSCO V1.3 through to the Australian targeted updates of 2021 and 2022, and to the new Australian classification when it becomes available. Concordances to the international standard classification of occupation (ISCO08) and the United States standard occupation classification (US SOC), which underpins the US occupational information network (O*NET) system, will also be developed and maintained by Stats NZ.
Submitters wanted Stats NZ to know that maintaining concordances was incredibly important. We received requests to ensure concordances back to classifications as the old New Zealand standard classification of occupation (NZSCO99), which was pre-ANZSCO.
I was pleased to see the intention to maintain international comparability through concordances, even with the move away from ANZSCO. This is crucial for ensuring that our labour market data remains relevant in a global context.
Ensuring the continuity and comparability of occupation classification data is essential, particularly for maintaining alignment with international standards and facilitating cross-border employment between New Zealand and Australia
A key requirement is the ability to maintain historical time series. We understand the trade-offs in balancing currency and relevancy against impacts on time series but would like to underscore the relevance of historical codes and data in time-series analysis for policy making and research.
While most submitters recognised the benefit of flexibility, that it enables more regular updates, some raised concerns about frequent changes. Submitters recommended that Stats NZ develop a robust and transparent process for how and when the list is updated and released.
[We have] concerns about the work and risks involved in managing a classification that is updated frequently.
We are comfortable with the proposal for potentially annual updates, but not more frequent than this. We understand that there is always a balance between changes that improve currency and relevancy and the impact of those changes to always concord well with previous versions, or to other classifications such as ISCO.
A classification that is constantly being updated adds costs to users in terms of their reworking. There is also the risk with each change in terms of users making errors in implementation. There may also be system costs if HR and payroll systems need frequent revision to accommodate new or changing occupation codes. To reduce the cost of transition to the new classification, Stats NZ should formalise an annual updating schedule.

#### Current use and issues with ANZSCO
Submitters told us of their experience with using current occupation classification (ANZSCO). They identified issues with the joint classification. These comments reiterated the need for a different approach to classifying occupations.
We support a classification that is easier to keep up to date. There are key occupations that are important for [us] that are not covered by ANZSCO v1.3.
Submitters agreed that ANZSCO is significantly out of date. Since it was released, the labour market and economy have changed, and the classification does not accurately reflect the contemporary reality in both countries.
ANZSCO codes are not updated to reflect current roles.
The existing version is out of date (aside from minor alterations, no major changes since 2006).
I couldn’t see information management, knowledge, modern digital workplace, and data governance occupations in the list. In this fast-paced digital world we live in, with data growing exponentially and the challenges and opportunities that brings, it would be good to see more representation of this discipline.
In 2021 and 2022 the ABS undertook Australian-targeted updates on ANZSCO, which Stats NZ chose not to participate in. Submitters noted that these reviews have resulted in more differences between NZ and Australian occupations and skill levels, which have caused issues.
Currently, navigating between different versions of ANZSCO can be confusing, and especially when certain occupations are categori[s]ed at different skill levels between the two countries (eg, Cooks are level 3 in the latest ANZSCO used in Australia but level 4 in New Zealand).
The current ANZCO numbers for our trade don’t align with NZ terminology for our trade.
Some submitters indicated their industries’ occupations were invisible in the current classification. It is challenging to find a suitable occupation code for what they do. This impacted their ability to find relevant data about themselves, their industry, or business, and made visa applications difficult when needing overseas workers.
There is a noted lack of coverage and finer detail of occupations, which creates confusion when deciding how to choose an occupation for a survey or for the census.
Clearer and more distinct occupational classification categories are needed. The broad use of the title ‘engineer’ in ANZSCO is an issue. [...] This makes it impossible to determine the actual number of qualified engineers in New Zealand, both domestically trained and from overseas, making efforts more difficult to assess the extent of skills shortages and address diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) issues. […] This blurring of distinctions between engineers and tradespeople does not help with our understanding of the true engineering workforce landscape in New Zealand.
A few submitters had concerns about the short timeframe for our consultation. One of the main concerns about the timeframe was the impact this had on the ability of industry representatives and Workforce Development Councils (WDCs) to work with their members to identify changes they would like to see in the proposed occupation list.
The length of consultation seems very short and begs the question if it allows for sufficient information and perspectives to be obtained to inform policy.
The outcome of this consultation could have significant ramifications for occupational classification in New Zealand. Therefore, stakeholders should have been afforded further time to ensure that more perspectives [were] gathered and considered.
Some submitters were concerned option 1 was highlighted as the preferred option and requested more detail around its application.
While the consultation paper puts forward [four] options, and specifies that option 1 is the preferred, the lack of further information, including the benefits/costs of the other options, makes it hard to compare and comment accurately on the impacts of each. This does suggest that the consultation intends to move forward with option 1 regardless.
The paper states that option 1 is the preferred option, but also asks submitters their thoughts on all four options outlined. However, Business NZ is disappointed to see that favouritism towards option 1 in the paper has effectively crowded out any real mention of the other three options to consider.

#### Concerns and questions raised
While there was little disagreement with option 1 (3 percent) there were concerns and thoughts raised, with the following themes.
Submitters sought reassurance on the dependency of Stats NZ’s new classification on Tahatū, and voiced reservations around the readiness of Tahatū and its longevity. Stats NZ will be responsible for developing and managing the occupation classification in Ariā, engaging with stakeholders, and providing governance. TEC will continue to manage and develop Tahatū as a careers advisory platform using their content development and stakeholder engagement processes and their own governance structure. Stats NZ is a stakeholder in the Tahatū process. Stats NZ will also continue to maintain the occupation classification if Tahatū no longer exists.
Prior to reading this Stats NZ consultation document, we were not aware of the work that TEC is undertaking with their new Online Careers Solution Platform – Tahatū – and are very concerned that this will also not make contemporary and evolving work opportunities, such as those that exist in our sector, visible to students.
We acknowledge the work TEC has done to improve the relevance of occupations to Aotearoa New Zealand but highlight that their work is not yet complete across many of the sectors in our coverage area. Additionally, not all of [the] occupations from Tahatū are listed on the proposed occupation changes to the ANZSCO descriptors.
We would expect that Stats would have assurances from the TEC that the Tahatū database is going to be maintained in the long term. Is this the case? For example, we would expect that there would need to be mitigations in place if in the future TEC were unable to maintain Tahatū.
The new classification is proposed to be a flat list of occupations. A flat list means there is no hierarchy built into the classification – instead, all occupations are presented in a single list at the most detailed level. This provides the flexibility to add new occupations at any time, as required. It also allows different output views to be created for different uses. Stats NZ will provide output views to the existing ANZSCO levels 1 to 4.
There was strong support for the proposed flat list and the flexibility this provides. However, many submitters made it clear that retaining the linkage to the existing ANZSCO level 1 to 4 hierarchy was highly important. Stats NZ will provide output views to the existing ANZSCO levels 1 to 4. This will allow data to be aggregated and compared at different levels of groupings of occupations. For example, at the major group level 1 managers, professional etc.
The current proposal for option 1 is to move to a flat list of occupations with no hierarchy built into the coding. As currently written in the consultation document, we have concerns and would not support. However, in our discussions with you, you’ve provided assurances that the existing six-digit code hierarchy would remain, ‘to the side’ as a means to identify, organise, and group occupations. Specifically, that it won’t alter the ability for users to work with occupations at consistent standard higher levels.
We find the occupation structure very helpful in our workforce analysis and planning. We support the proposed approach from Stats NZ to still have the existing (non-updated) level 1 to 4 structure alongside the flat file. If this was not the case, we would need to retrofit it ourselves, which comes with a cost to us.
We support the proposals for output views that allow flexibility to group and use occupations in potentially more user-relevant ways. But we would like to underscore the need for users not to lose the ability to use occupations under some hierarchic framework that is considered the national standard.

### Part B: Implementation considerations, concerns, and mitigations.
We asked submitters their thoughts on implementation considerations, concerns, and mitigations.
Themes and sub-themes from submissions are outlined below.

#### Issues affecting how the proposal would work in practice
Some submitters wanted more detail about how the new proposal would be maintained and updated in the future. They sought clarification on the engagement model and strategy of the ongoing governance of the new classification.
It would be useful to have an overall timeline/roadmap of proposed implementation and ongoing maintenance, and how governance will be practically managed.
It is important that the occupation codes accurately represent the sector. It would be good to understand what the process for this would be and how sector groups would be able to contribute to this as it evolves.
We emphasise the need for an effective engagement model and strategy to ensure the users of ANZSCO data and the industries who depend on it for workforce planning are able to be informed and have a voice as experts in a meaningful and effective way.
Some submitters had comments about the implementation of a new classification and the need for good documentation for this, including additions to the list and version control.
We would support the development and maintenance of good clear documentation and version control and would encourage concordances to contain a good, timestamped user history, including additions and deletions, changes in definitions and description, skill levels, as well as impacts on trend analysis and other usage changes. For example, if a new code is introduced – where people with this occupation may have been coded to in the past, or, if an occupation is deleted, that the rationale is available for users to see.
It is important that the process for developing and updating the classification system is transparent and inclusive. Providing clear communication, training, and resources will help all users adapt to the new system.
What is unclear from this proposal is how the need for future proofing will play a part in these reviews, and whether emerging roles will be able to be added to the list even if there are 0 current workers, or if a threshold will remain in order to capture the workforce at the time of the review.
Managing updates and changes to the occupation list needs to have a clear process, including notification to stakeholders to give time for adoption.
[A] recurring theme was the critical need for stability, flexibility, and predictability within the classification system. Irrespective of the chosen option, it is imperative to establish a clear, transparent process for managing updates and changes. This includes providing stakeholders with ample notice and support to adapt to new classifications, thus ensuring a smooth transition and minimi[s]ing potential disruptions.
The process for updating or adding new codes will need to be developed through consultation with relevant sector groups. This process of consultation will also need to extend to the occupation definitions attached to the codes.
Regular reviews and feedback mechanisms should be established to ensure the classification system remains relevant and effective in addressing New Zealand’s unique labour market needs.

#### Stats NZ and TEC relationship and responsibilities
Developing a memorandum of understanding (MoU) and a relationship agreement with TEC was supported by submitters. This will set out the roles and responsibilities of each department to ensure consistency for uses. TEC has confirmed in their submission that working arrangements including governance processes will be agreed as part of developing the MoU between TEC and Stats NZ.
Working arrangements including governance processes will be agreed as part of developing the memorandum of understanding between TEC and Stats NZ.
Given Tahatū is a project using government funding, sitting within the careers space where funding has both grown and shrunk over time depending on government priorities, it needs to be very clear as to what the involvement will be with its contribution to the occupation classification system and how this function will be protected in the future. Clear roles and responsibilities, and the relationship between various stakeholders in the system, should be mapped and agreed on in principle by stakeholders before any decisions are made.

#### Continued consultation
Submitters encouraged continued engagement with stakeholders around how updating occupations in each sector is important to keep the classification relevant and current.
It will be important to ensure there is a sound process in place to [continue] engaging with other stakeholders. This will be critical to get views on future changes to the classification and further understand user experiences.
We think that some more consultation with some sectors may need to be built into the update process, not necessarily every year but at least from time to time.
Regular reviews and feedback mechanisms should be established to ensure the classification system remains relevant and effective in addressing New Zealand’s unique labour market needs.
We have taken this on board and will continue consultation with key stakeholders and groups, as noted in Next steps .
Some risks identified were the ability for Stats NZ to make additions and changes without consultation, thus causing extra strain for industries to keep up.
[A risk is] that this proposal, though a good idea, will not address the issue with NZ being behind other countries to understand where the value from technology and creativity is occurring in the economy and society.
We are concerned that providing [Stats NZ] with the power to make changes/additions at any time may have its own disadvantages if not monitored properly. Transporting New Zealand urge[s] Stats NZ to consider how it can mitigate the risk with any changes.
While we applaud the intention of Stats NZ’s approach to connecting information sources across government and maximising the use [of] technologies for efficiency, this does come with risk to sectors such creative and tech where new occupations are a constant, and these need to be visible in data systems for government, industry, and the future workforce.
Some submitters were concerned about potential costs with implementation and ongoing updates, such as administrative costs. There were requests that Stats NZ makes sure these changes don’t create cost pressures on other agencies.
We would caution against high frequency of adjustment given potential administrative cost.
If Stats NZ [were] to progress with its preferred option, Stats would need to provide assurance that wider costs to the system could be met from within baselines and would not create cost pressures for other agencies.
Some submitters acknowledged that there may be a cost to their agencies with system changes required to implement. However, as they can do this in a time frame that suits them, they will manage this from their existing baselines.
Given the scope of the change, this would be a very large piece of work, requiring significant analysis, solution design, build/implementation, and testing resources. […] Our main feedback is that we will need a long lead-in time of around at least eight months for the change.
There would be some cost to our agency to do a minimal implementation to modify our systems to introduce the new/modified occupation coding system. We would deploy the code system via the service that we operate, and this would be a BAU cost.
The financial and resource implications of adopting a new classification system will be determined by the final decisions around and implementation dates of any new classification system. For preferred option 1, there are likely to be costs involved with IT system changes/updates and additionally reporting changes, which in some areas fall under BAU activity but in others will require extra work for which we can scope and plan accordingly.
Some submitters had comments on application programming interfaces (APIs), with needing a timeline for when they could be used so plans could be made to implement system changes.
Businesses will need to incur the set-up cost for the use of dynamic APIs. There is no indication of when this will be – businesses need to plan budgets and work programmes for ICT changes and a reasonable lead-in time will be required.
ABS and Stats NZ jointly announced the separation of the ANZCO today, in Comparable but tailored occupational classifications for Australia and New Zealand .
We can now confirm that we will be releasing a new New Zealand-focused occupation classification.
We’re proposing to name this classification the National Occupation List (NOL) :
- This name reflects that the new product is a flat list and complements the use of the term ‘Green List’ by Immigration NZ.
- The new list is user driven and for use across the NZ data system, hence the title of ‘national’.
- We made the call not to revert to the title NZSCO to avoid confusion with the previous classification and signal the modernised approach we are taking.
We’re seeking feedback on the proposed name and welcome any suggestions for improvement or new ideas.
The first version of the NOL is proposed to be released on 20 November 2024 .
We acknowledge the consultation period was shorter than for our usual consultation periods due to aligning timeframes with Australia. Due to this we have allowed more time for making any additions or changes for this first version of the NOL. If you would like to make a submission for the first release, you can access the form to make a request for change on About the National Occupation List .
Proposals for inclusion in the first version of the NOL will close at 5pm, 30 October 2024 . This can be a proposal for a new occupation or a change to an existing occupation, such as to the skill level or definition.
Please note this is the cut-off for the first-version release only. We will accept requests for changes at any time with a view to release a new version annually. More information about dates will be advised. We are seeking further feedback on the release date for the annual release schedule to ensure it is suitable for customers. There is no set time when agencies must be using the NOL; they can transition it into their processes at a time that is appropriate for them.
Send feedback on the new name and release schedule to occupations@stats.govt.nz by 30 October 2024 .
We intend to integrate the NOL with the Tertiary Education Commission (TEC) Tahatū platform. The first version of the NOL will be a standalone list, without links to Tahatū, until Tahatū is officially released.
We have identified areas that need more work:
- Engaging more with Māori to develop kaupapa Māori definitions: We will work with Māori stakeholders to determine the best way to progress this
- Developing the ongoing process for adding or changing occupations in the new classification: We will develop clear guidelines and work with stakeholders to make sure this process is robust and transparent.
- Reviewing user guidance and implementation support: We will continue to work closely with customers to understand how we can support the implementation of the new classification across the data system.
- Developing and maintaining concordances with ANZSCO v1.3, the new Australian occupation classification, and other international classifications, such as the international standard classification of occupation (ISCO): We will work with stakeholders to determine if any other concordances will be needed.
- Integrating with the Tertiary Education Commission (TEC) Tahatū platform: We will work with TEC to formalise the relationship and expectations of both parties so there are clear guidelines and processes to share content across platforms.
Qualitative analysis – the process of analysing, understanding, and interpreting meaning in non-numeric, textual data. This includes the analysis of naturally expressed opinions or views by people.
Thematic analysis – also known as topic analysis; a common form of qualitative analysis. It is used to identify meaningful patterns across a dataset.

### Terms related to standards and classifications
Classification – a statistical classification is a way to group a set of related categories in a meaningful, systematic, and standard format. The statistical classification is usually exhaustive, has mutually exclusive and well-described categories, and has either a hierarchical or a flat structure. A statistical classification usually contains codes and descriptors.
Data system – refers to people and organisations who collect and use data.
NZ Government data system map is an example.
Statistical standard – provides a comprehensive set of guidelines for surveys and administrative sources collecting information on a particular topic.
What are standards and classifications? has more information on statistical standards.

### Appendix 1: Options for an updated occupation classification and our intention to introduce separate but aligned occupation classifications for Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia

#### Option 1: Create an independent New Zealand occupation classification that leverages existing content
We propose developing an independent New Zealand classification that is aligned to Australia. Stats NZ is actively exploring a new approach that leverages the work done by Tertiary Education Commission (TEC) in developing their new Online Careers Solution Platform – Tahatū. Tahatū will be a publicly available careers information service that includes a database of information about occupations, tertiary education, and training.
We propose to use digital technology to link occupations in the updated occupation classification to the content and information on Tahatū – minimising duplication and providing a direct link from occupations to careers information. Stats NZ would be able to make timely updates to the occupation classification, enabling new and emerging occupations to be added to the classification more frequently. It would require significantly less resource and time than the other options due to the work already completed in the development of Tahatū.
The existing ANZSCO skill levels would be retained.
This is our preferred option.

#### Option 2: Adopt the Australian revision of ANZSCO v1.3
The ABS completed targeted updates of ANZSCO in 2021 and 2022. They are now currently undertaking a comprehensive review of ANZSCO. This review is being undertaken to ensure classifications reflect the contemporary Australian labour market and is driven by government and stakeholder priorities in Australia. Australia intends to have an updated occupation classification in place in time for their next census (in 2026).
Given the review’s focus on meeting Australia’s needs, it is unlikely that the resulting update will be fit for New Zealand’s labour force. Skill differences between the two countries, which have been evident for some time, could be magnified (for example, there are distinct differences in the skills required for New Zealand financial professionals, health and welfare support workers, and agricultural workers). If we were to adopt Australia’s upcoming revision, we would be using the same classification as Australia, but it would not be fit for purpose for the New Zealand labour market. We would also continue to experience the same challenges of maintaining joint classifications.

#### Option 3: Adapt the Australian revision of ANZSCO v1.3 to reflect New Zealand content in a joint classification
While Australia's comprehensive review of ANZSCO is focused on the Australian labour market, we have considered making further changes to Australia’s revised classification to reflect New Zealand content and maintain a joint classification. This would need to occur after Australia’s revision is finalised to avoid delaying their timeframes. We would need a significant amount of resource and funding to adapt the Australian revision to better reflect New Zealand’s labour force, and to assess whether it could then be a joint classification. As a first step, we would need to assess and understand all the updates and changes Australia is proposing. We could then consider those updates for New Zealand, and work with the ABS to consider how to integrate each of our respective changes in the joint classification. Stats NZ needs to undertake further consultation with the ABS to understand the feasibility of this option. Even with an updated joint classification, we would continue to experience the challenges of maintaining joint classifications.

#### Option 4: Update the current version of ANZSCO v1.3 for New Zealand-only classification
Stats NZ could undertake our own comprehensive review of the existing version of ANZSCO (v1.3) to update it for the New Zealand context and create a separate occupation classification. We would need to examine the overall structure of the classification, content, and definitions and engage with relevant stakeholders to ensure content reflected the contemporary New Zealand labour market. This approach would take time and require significant resources and funding. For example, the ABS is completing their review over two years and obtained AU$24 million to achieve this.
